summary: A 14th-century Ming Dynasty novel attributed to Luo Guanzhong, one of the Four Great Classical Novels of China. It details the conflict between the states of Wei, Shu, and Wu after the fall of the Han Dynasty during the Three Kingdoms period. The novel's protagonist is Liu Bei, founder of Shu, with Cao Cao serving as the primary antagonist. Major events include the Oath of the Peach Garden, the Battle of Red Cliffs, and the stratagems of Zhuge Liang (the 'Sleeping Dragon' or 'Crouching Dragon'). The most common version was compiled in 1679 with an opening line about the cyclical nature of empires. A proverb states that the old should not read this book.

works.name: Oath of the Peach Garden
works.description: The foundational scene of the novel in which Liu Bei, Guan Yu, and Zhang Fei swear brotherhood. They meet at a tavern, bring a black bull and white horse to the peach garden, and vow to die 'on the same day, in the same month, in the same year.' This occurs during the Yellow Turban Rebellion at the novel's opening. The oath is the most frequently referenced single event from the novel in quizbowl. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Three men (Liu Bei, Guan Yu, Zhang Fei) swear an oath in a peach garden to become sworn brothers
examples: Three men in this novel make an oath in a peach garden to become sworn brothers.
Determined to protect their nation from the Yellow Turban rebels, three characters in this novel swear an oath in a peach garden.
This novel opens with Liu Bei, Guan Yu, and Zhang Fei swearing the Oath of the Peach Garden.

clues.clue: Characters who met at a tavern bring a black bull and white horse to this place; they agree to die 'on the same day, in the same month, in the same year'
examples: Characters who met at a tavern bring a black bull and white horse to this place... those three men agree to die 'on the same day, in the same month, in the same year.'

clues.clue: The oath is sworn during the Yellow Turban Rebellion
examples: Determined to protect their nation from the Yellow Turban rebels, three characters in this novel swear an oath in a peach garden.
During the Yellow Turban rebellion in this work, Guan Zu, Zhang Fei and Liu Bei swear the Oath of the Peach Garden.

works.name: Battle of Red Cliffs
works.description: The climactic battle of the novel in which the allied forces of Sun Quan and Liu Bei defeat Cao Cao. Central stratagems include Zhuge Liang borrowing arrows using straw boats on a foggy night, summoning the southeast wind, and Pang Tong (Young Phoenix/Fledgling Phoenix) tricking Cao Cao into chaining his ships together. Guan Yu allows Cao Cao to live after the battle. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Zhuge Liang borrows arrows using straw boats on a foggy night (sometimes described as 'twenty boats covered with straw' or 'empty straw ships with lanterns')
examples: One character in this novel completes an impossible task with the help of a foggy night and twenty boats covered with straw.
empty straw ships with lanterns on are used to gather one hundred thousand arrows in one evening.
that character supposedly... 'borrows' arrows with straw boats to win the Battle of Red Cliffs.

clues.clue: Zhuge Liang summons the southeast wind for a fire attack
examples: one man plays a trick to borrow arrows and performs a ceremony to summon the eastern wind for a fire attack.
A summoned southeast wind allows Sun Quan and Liu Bei to defeat the forces of Cao Cao at the Battle of Red Cliffs.

clues.clue: Young Phoenix / Fledgling Phoenix (Pang Tong) tricks Cao Cao into chaining his ships together
examples: A character in this novel, who is later mistakenly killed after switching horses with his master, proposes chaining some ships together to deal with motion sickness.
One advisor to the protagonist, Young Phoenix, tricked the antagonist into chaining together his ships together for stability.

clues.clue: Two characters both write the word 'fire' in the palms of their hands
examples: Two characters in this novel coincidentally both write the word 'fire' in the palms of their hands.

clues.clue: Guan Yu (wielding the Green Dragon Crescent Blade, riding Red Hare) allows Cao Cao to live after the battle
examples: A man who wielded the Green Dragon Crescent Blade and rode Red Hare allowed that antagonist to live after the Battle of Red Cliffs.

works.name: Zhuge Liang's Stratagems
works.description: Zhuge Liang, nicknamed 'Sleeping Dragon' or 'Crouching Dragon,' is the most prominent strategist in the novel. He is recruited after Liu Bei visits his thatched cottage three times. His exploits include the empty fort strategy (playing a guqin at Xicheng with only 100 soldiers), the seven captures of Meng Huo, the Stone Sentinel Maze, praying to the Big Dipper at the Wuzhang Plains, and having a wooden statue of himself made to scare his rival after death. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Liu Bei visits Zhuge Liang's thatched cottage three times to recruit him (the 'Sleeping Dragon'/'Crouching Dragon'); Zhuge Liang then outlines a plan involving taking control of the Riverlands
examples: A sequence of three visits convinces one character in this novel to join a cause, after which he outlines a plan that involves first taking control of the Riverlands.
A leader in this novel visits the thatched cottage of a man nicknamed Sleeping Dragon three times in order to recruit him into that leader's service.

clues.clue: Captures and releases a barbarian leader (Meng Huo) seven times before he surrenders
examples: In this novel, a barbarian leader is captured and freed seven times before finally surrendering.
One character in this novel subdues and releases his enemy seven times until he pledges his allegiance.

clues.clue: Creates a wooden sculpture of himself whose appearance after his death causes his rival to flee
examples: A character in this novel creates a wooden sculpture of himself, whose appearance after his death causes his rival to flee with fear.

One character in this novel prays to the Big Dipper to relieve his illness but dies anyway at the Wuzhang Plains, although he has a wooden statue of himself made to make his rival think he is still alive.

clues.clue: Prays to the Big Dipper to relieve his illness but dies at the Wuzhang Plains
examples: One character in this novel prays to the Big Dipper to relieve his illness but dies anyway at the Wuzhang Plains.

clues.clue: Stone Sentinel Maze — his father-in-law leads a man out of the stone maze
examples: That character lays a Stone Sentinel Maze to discourage his enemy from pursuing him.
That character's father-in-law leads a man out of a stone maze.

clues.clue: Empty fort strategy: defends Xicheng with 100 soldiers and plays a guqin to bluff Sima Yi
examples: The town of Xicheng is defended by 100 soldiers and Zhuge Liang playing a guqin in this work.
Name this strategy employed by Zhuge Liang to trick Sima Yi after the Battle of Jieting.

works.name: Cao Cao's Deeds
works.description: Cao Cao serves as the primary antagonist. He is depicted as cunning and ruthless. He claims to enjoy killing in his sleep (and kills a servant to prove it), sets 'chicken ribs' as a password, slaughters a household after a misunderstanding about cooking a pig, and tells Liu Bei they are the only two real heroes (causing Liu Bei to pretend to be scared by lightning). He nearly succeeds in assassinating the tyrant Dong Zhuo early in the novel. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Claims to like killing people while asleep; kills a servant trying to put a blanket on him to prove it
examples: That character in this novel claims that he likes to kill people while asleep, killing a servant trying to put a blanket on him to prove his point.

clues.clue: Sets 'chicken ribs' as a password; enraged when subordinate correctly interprets its meaning
examples: One character in this novel sets 'chicken ribs' as a password and is enraged when his subordinate correctly interprets its meaning.

clues.clue: Slaughters a house full of people after mistaking plans to cook a pig as plans to kill him
examples: One character in this work slaughters a house full of people after mistaking plans to cook a pig as plans to kill him.

clues.clue: Tells Liu Bei they are the only two real heroes, causing Liu Bei to pretend to be scared by lightning
examples: One character in this novel is spooked when another character tells him that they are the only two real heroes, but pretends to have been scared by lightning.

clues.clue: Nearly succeeds in assassinating the tyrant Dong Zhuo
examples: In his most famous novel, Cao Cao nearly succeeds in assassinating Dong Zhuo.

works.name: Liu Bei's Character
works.description: Liu Bei is the novel's protagonist, founder of Shu. He is known for benevolent leadership — but also for extreme gestures such as throwing his infant son to the ground after Zhao Yun risks his life to save the baby. He claims to enjoy his new life too much to think of home (the 'lezhong bu si shu' incident). A hunter once serves him the flesh of his own wife, claiming it was wolf meat.

clues.clue: Throws his infant son to the ground after a general (Zhao Yun, one of the 'Five Tigers') risks his life to save the baby
examples: Another character in this novel tosses his infant son on the ground after one of his generals had risked his life to save the baby.
One protagonist saves an infant whose mother throws herself down a well; that infant is subsequently cast down by his father.

clues.clue: Claims to enjoy his new life too much to think about his former home
examples: In one scene in this novel, a character claims to enjoy his new life too much to think about his former home.

clues.clue: A hunter claims to have served wolf's meat, but Liu Bei discovers it was the flesh of the hunter's wife
examples: A hunter in this novel claims to his guest that he has served wolf's meat, but the guest realizes the day after that it was in fact the flesh of his wife.

clues.clue: Liu Bei is the founder and protagonist of Shu; Guan Yu plays Go while being treated for a poison arrow wound
examples: If anyone can be said to be the protagonist of Romance of the Three Kingdoms, it's this founder of the kingdom of Shu.
While being treated for a poison arrow wound, Guan Yu pretends to be unhurt by doing this activity [playing Go].

works.name: Other Characters and Episodes
works.description: Various other notable characters and events from the novel. Pang Tong ('Young Phoenix'/'Fledgling Phoenix') is killed in an ambush at the Valley of the Fallen Phoenix after switching horses with his master. Guan Yu defeats 6 generals to pass through 5 passes. The Red Hare horse is given to Lu Bu by the warlord Dong Zhuo. The novel includes the coalition against Dong Zhuo (led by Yuan Shao), and Dong Zhuo's death at the hands of his foster son Lu Bu over the beauty Diao Chan. One character receives an empty food box from his superior, interpreting it as an order to kill himself.

clues.clue: Fledgling Phoenix / Young Phoenix (Pang Tong) killed in ambush at Valley of the Fallen Phoenix after switching horses with his master
examples: In this novel, the Fledgling Phoenix is killed in an ambush at the Valley of the Fallen Phoenix after switching horses with his master.
A character in this novel, who is later mistakenly killed after switching horses with his master, proposes chaining some ships together.

clues.clue: Guan Yu defeats 6 generals to pass through 5 passes
examples: One general in this work defeats 6 other generals in order to pass through 5 passes.

clues.clue: The Red Hare horse is given to Lu Bu by Dong Zhuo; Dong Zhuo seizes Luoyang after the death of Emperor Ling
examples: The Red Hare is given to the greatest warrior of the warlord Dong Zhuo, who had earlier seized Luoyang after the death of Emperor Ling.
A dispute involving Diao Chan causes his death at the hands of his foster son Lu Bu.

clues.clue: One character receives an empty food box and interprets it as an order to kill himself
examples: One character in this novel receives an empty food box from his superior, which he interprets as an order to kill himself.

clues.clue: Yuan Shao-led coalition against the tyrant Dong Zhuo, who takes power after the Yellow Turban Rebellion
examples: Liu Bei, Cao Cao, and Sun Jian take part in the Yuan Shao-led coalition against this tyrant, who takes power after the Yellow Turban Rebellion.

works.name: General / Identification
works.description: How the novel is identified in clues. It is a 14th-century Chinese novel attributed to Luo Guanzhong, one of the Four Great Classical Novels of China. It details the conflict between the states of Wei, Shu, and Wu after the fall of the Han Dynasty. The most common version was compiled in 1679 and added an opening line about the cyclical nature of empires ('what is long divided must unite; what is long united must divide'). Begins with the Yellow Turban Rebellion led by Zhang Jue and ends with Sima Yan establishing the Jin Dynasty. A proverb says the young should not read Water Margin and the old should not read this book. Luo Guanzhong may also have authored or edited Water Margin. Lu Xun was inspired by this novel.

clues.clue: Novel by Luo Guanzhong about the conflict between Wei, Shu, and Wu

this Chinese classic about the struggle for supremacy between Wei, Shu, and Wu.

clues.clue: Set after the fall of the Han Dynasty / during the Yellow Turban Rebellion

examples: The most common version of this novel was one compiled in 1679 that, among other major revisions, added an opening line describing the cyclical nature of empires.
opening lines note that what's 'long divided, must unite' and what's 'long united, must divide.'

examples: According to a proverb, the young should not read Water Margin, while the old should not read this book.

examples: Romance of the Three Kingdoms is by this 14th century Chinese author, who may also have written its fellow Great Classical Novel Water Margin.

clues.clue: Referenced in Magic the Gathering cards (Portal: Three Kingdoms set) including Peach Garden Oath, Riding Red Hare, Borrowing 10,000 Arrows, Empty City Ruse
examples: Cards like 'Riding the Dilu Horse' and 'Empty City Ruse' reference this work... name this event referenced by the cards 'Peach Garden Oath' and 'Cao Cao, Lord of Wei.'

clues.clue: In Akutagawa's 'The Nose,' Father Zenchi compares himself to Liu Bei from this novel
examples: In 'The Nose,' Father Zenchi compares himself to Liu Bei, a character from this 14th-century Chinese novel about the wars at the end of the Han dynasty.

comprehensive_summary:
Romance of the Three Kingdoms is a 14th-century Ming Dynasty novel attributed to Luo Guanzhong, and one of the Four Great Classical Novels of China. It chronicles the conflict between the states of Wei, Shu, and Wu following the collapse of the Han Dynasty. The most commonly read version was compiled in 1679, which added the famous opening line about the cyclical nature of empires: 'what is long divided must unite; what is long united must divide.' The novel begins with the Yellow Turban Rebellion led by Zhang Jue and ends with Sima Yan establishing the Jin Dynasty.

The protagonist is Liu Bei, founder of Shu, whose sworn brotherhood with Guan Yu and Zhang Fei in the Oath of the Peach Garden is the novel's most iconic scene. They meet at a tavern, bring a black bull and white horse to the peach garden, and vow to die on the same day. The primary antagonist is Cao Cao of Wei, characterized as ruthless and cunning — he claims to enjoy killing in his sleep and proves it by killing a servant, sets 'chicken ribs' as a password, slaughters a household over a misunderstanding about pig-cooking, and tells Liu Bei they are the only two real heroes (causing Liu Bei to feign fright from lightning). Cao Cao nearly assassinates the tyrant Dong Zhuo early in the novel.

The novel's strategic centerpiece is the Battle of Red Cliffs, where Zhuge Liang borrows arrows using straw boats on a foggy night, summons the southeast wind for a fire attack, and the Young Phoenix (Pang Tong) tricks Cao Cao into chaining his ships. Guan Yu later allows Cao Cao to escape. Zhuge Liang, nicknamed Sleeping Dragon or Crouching Dragon, is recruited after Liu Bei visits his thatched cottage three times. His other exploits include the empty fort strategy at Xicheng (bluffing Sima Yi by playing a guqin with only 100 soldiers), the seven captures and releases of the barbarian leader Meng Huo, the Stone Sentinel Maze, praying to the Big Dipper at the Wuzhang Plains, and having a wooden statue made of himself to frighten his rival after death.

Notable episodes include Liu Bei throwing his infant son to the ground after Zhao Yun risked his life to save the baby, Liu Bei claiming to enjoy his new life too much to think of home, a hunter serving Liu Bei the flesh of his own wife while claiming it was wolf meat, Guan Yu defeating six generals to pass through five passes, and Guan Yu playing Go while being treated for a poison arrow wound. Pang Tong is killed at the Valley of the Fallen Phoenix after switching horses with his master. The Red Hare horse is given to Lu Bu by Dong Zhuo, and Dong Zhuo is later killed by Lu Bu over the beauty Diao Chan.

A Chinese proverb states that the young should not read Water Margin and the old should not read this book. Luo Guanzhong is also credited with editing or co-authoring Water Margin. Lu Xun was inspired by this novel; the protagonist of his story 'Storm in a Teacup' obsessively rereads it. In Akutagawa's 'The Nose,' Father Zenchi compares himself to Liu Bei. The novel is also referenced extensively in the Magic the Gathering Portal: Three Kingdoms card set.

Three Kingdoms period — the historical period; has appeared as its own answerline (T6)
